title: W3: Tokenizers
blurb: Highest priority. BPE hand-computation appears in 2024 and 2026, and tokenizer-model coupling appears in 2025.

knowledge_points.title: Byte Pair Encoding by Hand
knowledge_points.recap: Be able to run two BPE iterations manually: count adjacent symbol pairs, merge the most frequent pair, update the vocabulary, and apply merges in learned order.

questions.q:
Consider the Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenizer discussed in class, with unicode characters as the initial vocabulary. After pretokenization on a text corpus, we obtain the following word frequency list:

$("pek", 3), ("pep", 3), ("eck", 4), ("eco", 4)$

1. Apply the first two training iterations of BPE to the word frequency list above and state the updated vocabulary at each iteration. (2 points)
questions.answer:
**The initial vocabulary is:**

$[c,e,k,o,p]$

**Iteration 1:** The most frequent pair is **$e+c$**, appearing in $eck(4)+eco(4)=8$ times. Merging **$e+c\to ec$**, we get the updated vocabulary: **$[c,e,k,o,p,ec]$ (1 point)**.

**Iteration 2:** The most frequent pair is now **$p+e$**, appearing in $pek(3)+pep(3)=6$ times. Merging **$p+e\to pe$**, we get updated vocabulary: **$[c,e,k,o,p,ec,pe]$ (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: count pairs with word frequencies. WS26 merges $e+c$ then $p+e$. SS24 variant on "aabbaacaaba" merges $aa\to X$, then $Xb\to Z$, vocabulary size 5.

questions.q:
Using the tokenizer learned above:

2. Tokenize the word `pecs`. (2 points)
questions.answer:
Split `pecs` into base characters:

`p e c s`

The character **`s` is not in the base vocabulary**, so we replace it by **`<unk>` (1 point)**:

`p e c <unk>`

Applying the learned merge rules in order **(1 point)**:

**Iteration 1 ($e+c\to ec$):** `p ec <unk>`

**Iteration 2 ($p+e\to pe$):** `p` and `e` are no longer adjacent, skip.

Final tokenization: **`[p, ec, <unk>]`**. If the merge rules were applied in the wrong order, iteration 2 before iteration 1, we would get the incorrect result `[pe, c, <unk>]`.
questions.extend: Memory: BPE inference applies learned merges in training order. Do not greedily apply a later merge first.

questions.q: 2. How is the number of training iterations of a BPE tokenizer determined in practice? (1 point)
questions.answer: In practice, **the vocabulary size is set before training** the BPE tokenizer. Training runs until **the desired vocabulary size is reached (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: target vocab size determines number of BPE merges.

knowledge_points.title: Pretokenization, Vocabulary Size, and Model Coupling
knowledge_points.recap: Tokenizers affect both sequence length and model parameters. A pretrained model embedding matrix is tied to the tokenizer that created its token IDs.

questions.q:
Tokenizers convert raw text into sequences of integer IDs, for example by repeatedly merging the most frequent pairs of symbols, as in the byte pair encoding tokenizer discussed in class.

1. Why can using a different tokenizer at inference time than the one used during pretraining lead to poor performance in a language model? (1 point)
questions.answer: **The input embedding matrix of a pretrained model is tied to the tokenizer it was trained with (1 point).** Each token ID corresponds to a specific row in the embedding matrix. Using a different tokenizer will produce different token IDs, which will not align with the embedding matrix and can lead to poor performance. To use a different tokenizer, one would need to retrain the model from scratch or adapt the embedding layer, for example by using adapters.
questions.extend: Memory: tokenizer IDs are the interface to the embedding matrix. Change IDs -> wrong rows.

questions.q: 2. You are given an English input text and train two tokenizers. Tokenizer A is trained on the text after applying pre-tokenization. Tokenizer B is trained directly on the raw text, without any pre-tokenization. In both cases, you apply byte pair encoding until no pair of symbols occurs more than once. Which tokenizer will likely produce a larger vocabulary, and why? (2 points)
questions.answer: **Tokenizer B will likely produce a larger vocabulary (1 point).** This is because in Tokenizer A, byte pair encoding is applied only within each pre-token, e.g. within each word, not across pre-token boundaries. Tokenizer B works on raw text, so BPE can merge characters across any boundary, including punctuation. For example, different variants of the same word with punctuation, like `dog.`, `dog!`, and `dog?`, are treated as separate patterns, and if they occur frequently, are added as different tokens in the vocabulary **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: pre-tokenization restricts merge boundaries, usually reducing weird cross-word/punctuation vocabulary growth.

questions.q: 4. Tokenizer A is trained on natural language text, and tokenizer B on programming code. Both tokenizers are used to tokenize the same programming code. Which tokenizer will produce a shorter sequence of tokens and why? (2 points)
questions.answer: **The tokenizer trained specifically on programming code will produce a shorter sequence of tokens (1 point).** This is because the code-specific tokenizer can tokenize common code patterns, keywords, and structures more efficiently. It can represent these elements as single tokens, whereas a tokenizer trained on natural language text might break them down into multiple subwords or characters, resulting in longer sequences **(1 point)**. Just mentioning that training and applying to the same data is more efficient is not an argument.
questions.extend: Memory: domain-matched tokenizer -> shorter token sequence. WS26 vocabulary-size variant: smaller vocab gives longer sequences, and attention FLOPs scale quadratically in length.

knowledge_points.title: Tokenizer Implementation from Notebook
knowledge_points.recap: Week 3 tutorial/homework implements a byte-level BPE tokenizer with GPT-2 pre-tokenization and special-token handling.

questions.q:
Tokenizer homework. Implement `train(text, vocab_size)` for a byte-level BPE tokenizer. It starts with 256 byte tokens, stores merge rules in `self.merges`, stores token-id-to-bytes in `self.vocab`, and uses GPT-2 pre-tokenization.

What are the key steps of the training loop?
questions.answer:
The training loop should **pre-tokenize with `GPT2_SPLIT_PATTERN`**, encode each pre-token to UTF-8 bytes, and represent each byte as its integer id. It then repeatedly **counts adjacent pair frequencies**, chooses the most frequent pair, assigns the next token id, and **merges that pair in all token sequences**.

It must update **`self.merges[(p0,p1)] = new_id`** and **`self.vocab[new_id] = self.vocab[p0] + self.vocab[p1]`**.
questions.extend: Memory: BPE training = pre-tokenize, count pairs, merge most frequent pair, store merge and vocab bytes.

questions.q:
Tokenizer homework. Implement `encode(text)` and `decode(ids)`. The special token `<|endoftext|>` must be treated as one token outside the BPE algorithm.

What should encode/decode do, and how are special tokens handled?
questions.answer:
`encode` should **split around allowed special tokens**, encode ordinary segments as UTF-8 byte ids, then apply learned BPE merges in merge-rank order. `<|endoftext|>` is emitted as **one dedicated id**, not decomposed or merged.

`decode` should **look up token ids in `self.vocab`**, concatenate byte strings, and UTF-8 decode the result. Special-token ids map back to their literal special-token bytes.
questions.extend: Memory: special tokens bypass BPE; ordinary text uses bytes plus learned merges.
